title: The GitOps Model
description:
## How Does GitOps Deliver Changes?

A team adopts **GitOps** for their Kubernetes cluster using a tool like Argo CD or Flux. Which statement best describes how their declarative configuration actually reaches the cluster?

options.text: A Git repository is the single source of truth for the desired declarative state, and an in-cluster agent continuously **pulls** from Git and reconciles the live cluster to match it
Engineers run `kubectl apply` by hand from their laptops whenever a change is needed, and Git is only used as a backup
The CI server SSHes into each node and edits the running configuration directly
The cluster polls Docker Hub and applies whatever the newest image's defaults happen to be

explanation: **GitOps** makes a **Git repository the single source of truth** for declarative infrastructure and app configuration. An **in-cluster agent** (Argo CD, Flux) continuously watches that repo, compares the desired state in Git to the live state in the cluster, and **reconciles** any drift — a *pull-based* model. Because every change is a reviewed, audited Git commit, the cluster is reproducible and you can roll back by reverting. Manual `kubectl apply` from a laptop (b) bypasses Git as the source of truth and causes drift; (c) and (d) are not declarative reconciliation at all.

hints.title: Source of truth + reconciliation
hints.body: Two ideas define GitOps: Git holds the *desired* state, and an agent inside the cluster constantly works to make the *actual* state match it.
